channel.title: Comments for page "What Passes as Normal in the Digital Age"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "What Passes as Normal in the Digital Age"

item.title: Nice.
item.content:encoded: Loved it, I actually hate AWCY? but GAW seemed like both serious and stupidly fun, and this helps create the personalities of most characters, I just loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: man i love this tale so much i've read it like three times and it's wonderful every time great job!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is so nice to read a tale about magic people doing funny things. I really like the characterizations as well, GAW are very quickly becoming my favourite group of merrymakers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GAW is always really fun to read. Well written GAW stuff is even better. I just love how they're more or less just kind of normal people. At least, living somewhat normal lives still. GAW are a bunch of weird, funny dorks and I love them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have such a giant soft spot for wholesome stories about trans women that I should probably never be trusted to give serious critique here, but for what it's worth I happy cried from reading this tale and I'd like to thank the author for creating it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved the tale, quick question/confusion though.

The note was nearly impossible to read, but she was able to glean meaning after a few reread. If she kept it at her desk, it would make people react to the, well, anomalous things that her other gifts might cause to be ignored by the regular people. Theoretically, he said it could also let her get away with doing weird things, same magic used in Mr. Normie.

That reads to me that it would make people react to the anomalous things that her other gifts might cause to be ignored. By the end it was clearer that it was causing the anomalous things to be ignored but the wording at this part made it seem it would cause people to react to otherwise ignored things?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to say that as a trans girl in a long distance relationship this hit a lot of feelings in me. And I'm honestly elated that one of my favorite SCPs (Eli and Lyris) is now involved in GAW. So in all honesty - thank you for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparently I never commented on this the first time I read it but I really love this. A lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still a nice tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was hesitant about GAW when it was first raised, but (as I've said before and no doubt will again) it is writing like this that earns GAW its place in the GoI roster. Within a few months it has better rounded characters than a host of other groups, and it serves an excellent purpose of solving the "why do all the professional scientists sound like teenage/twenties net users" dilemma that affects some of the writing for the site. This is warm and human and well-constructed - lovely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is adorable. These characters all feel very real. And they're a bunch of dorks. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, that is much appreciated!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also Andressa. So four, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhm, the normie, LYRE and Bluntfield?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Andy/Andressa is referred to as both "she" and "they", which is confusing.

As the only normie in the group, Dahlia might come in handy if the Foundation ever catches up to GAW. The can't SCP her, she's not anomalous, and only lolfoundation would kill off a random bystander.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is character assassination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
heres the comic you should write
jfb howd you get so good
this is so good
plus one id rate it more if i had a botnet youre my faovirte writer i love you
COMMENT*
not comic
sorry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dahlia is hetcopogg, who cannot do any magic. Bluntfiend/Jude is the magic one. They are both trans though. Also none of them use neo-pronouns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized that I hadn't realized that Dahlia/bluntfiend was a 'normie'. Didn't ze make the Stephen King meme in an earlier work?
Just something to consider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was neutral-voting last night because the writing is a bit hard to get through when it gets off on divergences and tangents. Granted, that's more or less how my thoughts work as well so it's understandable to portray that for a character, but it's rarely easy to latch onto or get through. However, I'm upvoting because this is another nice tale for the group, showing off very well how a non-anomalous person would work in the Foundaionverse where her friends are anomalous. It also introduces more trans characters in the Foundationverse, which I think the site needs. Most trans characters on the site (the very scant few we have) are part of the Foundation, so it's always good to see trans characters outside of there, particularly in a more grounded environment. I will support these tales with more trans characters and people writing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liking this a lot. GAW seemed like an extended joke at first (sic semper cannabis), and has developed into a recognizable, affecting part of the Foundationverse.

Seconding Freudian's comment above. Aiden Eldritch as well. The tales here are among the better fiction I've read online.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My heart is warmed as shit right now.

Seriously, this is really great characterization for GAW (especially Eli, what a cute and thoughtful half of an alien killsat) and it really goes a long way towards making a fictional internet community feel real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little iffy about how the writing at the start of this seems very stop. Start, as well as how the middle portion consists of a rather copious amount of references. I get it, that's part of GAW's schtick. It doesn't change the fact that people out of the loop, perhaps even some in, will feel a tad lost.

But I second most of the points above. It's heartwarming and continues the trend of adding depth to the charming ensemble contained within Gamers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tale just leaves me smiling. I'm glad that in a dystopian world full of ghouls and paramilitary ghostbusters and mad science that people can still have happy birthdays with the people most important to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D'nawwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww. Again, GAW works really well just as a slice of life group, probably with how grounded the group tries to be in typical modern day, and I'm enjoying the character studies of the group to an unreasonable degree.

ETA: Kinch, your consistent talent is making me give a shit about people, and it's used to good effect here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love! It made me smile repeatedly.

Think you left out a word:

trying to under the swirls that didn't make any sense

should it be "understand"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been wanting to write a tale about the only "normie" in GAW ever since I gave all the chat personalities. So here it is. With this tale, I wanted to show the very real friendships between these people, but also I wanted to talk about trans stuff.

And yes, this article means bluntfiend is canon trans man.

Edit: Is three trans characters in one article an SCP record, you think?
